title: Thirty-Day Emergency Readmission Rate by Department

landmines.name: exact-30-day-inclusive
landmines.kills: naive/1_exclusive_window.sql -- a strict < 30 days window misses a readmission that lands exactly 30 days after discharge (inclusive boundary)

landmines.name: planned-return-counted
landmines.kills: naive/2_any_admit_type.sql -- counting the next admission regardless of admit_type treats a planned ELECTIVE or internal TRANSFER return as a readmission, over-counting

landmines.name: zero-eligible-department-dropped
landmines.kills: naive/3_drop_zero_denominator.sql -- aggregating only over the eligible index set drops a department with zero eligible index stays (all-EXPIRED Palliative Care) instead of reporting NULL, and without NULLIF risks a divide-by-zero

landmines.name: unbounded-selfjoin-anylater
landmines.kills: naive/4_unbounded_selfjoin_slow.sql -- pairing an index with ANY later admission within 30 days (not the immediate next) over-counts bounce-backs, and the O(n^2) self-join is the designated TLE-slow solution

notes: An eligible index stay is completed (discharge_ts IS NOT NULL) and not a death or transfer-out (discharge_disposition NOT IN ('EXPIRED','TRANSFER')). A readmission is the patient's IMMEDIATE next admission (by admit_ts, admission_id) that is EMERGENCY and whose admit_ts is within 30 days INCLUSIVE of the index discharge. Rate = readmitted / eligible per index-stay department, NULLIF-guarded; a department with zero eligible index stays reports NULL and must still appear (LEFT JOIN from the department list). Rows are not time-sorted and admission_id is not chronological across patients, so sequencing must be by admit_ts via LEAD. reference.sql is the SQLite form (datetime(...,'+30 days')); reference.postgres.sql / reference.mysql.sql / reference.duckdb.sql give the per-engine 30-day interval. mssql omitted (DATEADD/DATEDIFF form, not verified in the authoring sandbox). TLE: at black scale (~1.3M admissions) the naive/4 unbounded self-join blows the limit while the LEAD window finishes well under it.
